1.pericope_label: Jesus, Mediator of a Better Covenant
1.synthesis: The main point of what is being said: we have such a high priest who has taken his seat at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in heaven, a minister in the holy places — in the true tent that the Lord set up, not man (vv.1–2). For every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices; it is necessary for this priest also to have something to offer (v.3). Now if he were on earth, he would not be a priest at all, since there are priests who offer gifts according to the law — they serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things, for when Moses was about to erect the tabernacle, he was instructed: "See that you make everything according to the pattern shown you on the mountain" (vv.4–5; Exodus 25:40). But as it is, Christ has obtained a ministry that is as much more excellent than the old as the covenant he mediates is better, since it is enacted on better promises (v.6).
1.historical_context: Chrysostom on v.1: "Paul mixes the lowly things with the lofty, ever imitating his Master, so that the lowly become the path to the lofty." The kephalaion (the main point, v.1) of the entire letter is the seated high priest — not standing to repeat his service but seated because his work is done. MKT notes that the "true tabernacle set up by the Lord" draws on the widespread Jewish apocalyptic tradition of the heavenly sanctuary (1 Enoch 14:8-25; T. Levi 3:4-8; 2 Baruch 4:3) and on the Exodus tradition that Moses built according to the heavenly pattern (Exodus 25:40). The claim that if Christ were on earth he would not be a priest (v.4) is a frank acknowledgment that Jesus had no priestly credentials within the Aaronic system — his priesthood operates in the realm where the earthly priesthood is only a copy.
1.literary_structure: Chrysostom identifies the structure of the letter as moving from Priest → Sacrifice → Covenant: the author has established the high priest's superiority (chs. 1–7), now establishes the sanctuary (the true tent, vv.1–5), and then the covenant (vv.6–13, the Jeremiah oracle). The central point is stated in v.6: more excellent ministry = better covenant = better promises. The argument from the Platonic shadow-substance distinction (copy and shadow vs. the original, v.5) is drawn from the Exodus mandate itself: God told Moses to build according to the heavenly pattern — which implies that the earthly tabernacle was always intended as a derivative representation, not the reality itself.
1.theological_application: Gill on v.5: the priests "serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things — things respecting the person, office, and grace of Christ; the priests themselves were types of him; the places they ministered in were an exemplar of the heavenly places, as the word may be rendered, where Christ is; and the things they ministered were shadows of the good things which Christ brings and gives." The shadow is not false — it genuinely represents the real; but the shadow's function is to point beyond itself, and to cling to the shadow when the substance has arrived is a misunderstanding of what the shadow was for. Gill on v.6: Christ "has now obtained a more excellent ministry... Christ has a ministry, he is the minister of the sanctuary, he has 'obtained' this ministry of his Father; he was called unto it and engaged in it by him; and he has 'now' obtained it; for though he was called to it from eternity, it was in time he came as an high priest."
1.christology: The ministry Christ exercises is characterized by its location (the true tent, the heavenly sanctuary), its quality (more excellent), and its covenant-basis (better promises). The earthly high priest's ministry was localized, annual, and based on the Sinaitic covenant with its conditional blessings. Christ's ministry is heavenly (above any terrestrial space), permanent (he has taken his seat — not standing to repeat), and based on the new covenant of Jer 31 whose promises are unconditional from God's side. The "better promises" of v.6 are identified in the following verses (8:10–12): the law written on hearts, universal knowledge of God, and complete forgiveness — precisely what the Sinaitic covenant's rituals could not provide but could only anticipate.

7.synthesis: If the first covenant had been faultless, there would have been no occasion to look for a second (v.7). But God found fault with them and promised a new covenant: "the days are coming when I will establish a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah — not like the covenant I made with their fathers on the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of Egypt, for they did not continue in my covenant, and so I showed no concern for them" (vv.8–9; Jeremiah 31:31–32). The new covenant: "I will put my laws into their minds and write them on their hearts; I will be their God and they shall be my people. No longer shall they teach their neighbor, for they shall all know me, from the least to the greatest. I will be merciful toward their iniquities and remember their sins no more" (vv.10–12; Jeremiah 31:33–34). In speaking of a new covenant, God made the first old, and what is becoming obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish (v.13).
7.historical_context: Jeremiah's new covenant oracle (Jeremiah 31:31–34) was written during the Babylonian exile — the first covenant had failed (Israel had broken it through persistent idolatry) and Jeremiah promises a future covenant in which God writes his law on hearts rather than stone tablets. MKT notes that the promise "no longer shall each one teach his neighbor" was not eliminating the teaching office but describing the transformation of how divine knowledge is transmitted: from external mediation (priests, teachers, scribes) to inward direct knowledge — every member of the covenant knowing God personally. The final clause — "I will remember their sins no more" — is the oracle's theological summit: not annual reminder-of-sins (as the Day of Atonement was) but permanent non-remembrance.
7.literary_structure: The section's logic is elegant: the very word "new" in Jeremiah's oracle implies the "old" — by promising something new, God declared the first covenant's obsolescence. The Jeremiah citation is the longest single OT quotation in the NT and functions as the covenant-charter for the letter's argument. The four promises of the new covenant (inward law, covenant relationship, universal knowledge, complete forgiveness) correspond to the four failures of the old covenant under which the people broke their agreement, lived as strangers to God's law, failed to know him, and accumulated unforgiven sin. The new covenant's promises address each failure directly.
7.theological_application: Gill on v.12: "I will be merciful to their unrighteousness — that is, sin; for all unrighteousness is sin, being contrary to the justice of God, and his righteous law: and the phrase is expressive of God's forgiveness of it, which is a very considerable article of the covenant of grace; mercy is the spring and original of pardon." The forgiveness promised in the new covenant is not the annual "covering" of the Aaronic sacrifices (which were repeated year after year because they did not perfectly remove sin-consciousness) but a permanent divine non-remembrance — a declaration that the account has been closed. Gill on v.10: the writing of the law on hearts is "not the moral law only, or the ceremonial law, but the whole of the law of Christ, the law of faith, and the rule of love, and the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus" — the covenant's interiority extends beyond moral principles to the dynamic of new-covenant spiritual life.
7.christology: Hebrews' citation of the entire Jeremiah oracle makes an implicit christological claim: this is the covenant that Christ mediates (v.6), and its promises are better because they are enacted through his better sacrifice. The old covenant's repeated sacrifices produced repeated reminders of sin (10:3); the new covenant's "I will remember their sins no more" depends on a sacrifice that permanently removes rather than annually covers. The single phrase "I will remember their sins no more" is the theological conclusion toward which chapters 9–10 will build: it is possible only because "by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified" (10:14). The new covenant's charter in Jeremiah was written into the future; its fulfillment is in Christ.
